This is a fundamental domain model change that splits the Shipment aggregate into a Shipment aggregate root with child Parcel entities, each owning its own lifecycle.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment & Tracking — responsible for the lifecycle of shipments from dispatch through final delivery confirmation, including individual parcel tracking, partial delivery detection, and delivery dispute resolution.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"An aggregate root representing the entire consignment sent to a single recipient address. Contains one or more Parcels. Has a composite status derived from its Parcels' statuses. Owns the shipping address, sender, and service level."},{"term":"Parcel","definition":"A child entity within a Shipment representing a single physical package. Each Parcel has its own tracking number, weight, dimensions, status, and tracking history. A Parcel cannot exist outside a Shipment."},{"term":"Tracking Number","definition":"A unique identifier assigned to each Parcel (not the Shipment) that enables status lookups. The Shipment may retain a group tracking reference, but operational tracking happens at Parcel level."},{"term":"Parcel Status","definition":"The current lifecycle state of an individual Parcel: CREATED, PICKED_UP, IN_TRANSIT, OUT_FOR_DELIVERY, DELIVERED, FAILED_DELIVERY_ATTEMPT, LOST, RETURNED_TO_SENDER."},{"term":"Shipment Status","definition":"A derived (computed) status reflecting the aggregate state of all Parcels: PENDING (all CREATED), IN_TRANSIT (any in transit), PARTIALLY_DELIVERED (some delivered, some not), DELIVERED (all delivered), FAILED (all failed/lost/returned). This is NOT stored independently — it is projected from Parcel statuses."},{"term":"Partial Delivery","definition":"A state where at least one but not all Parcels in a Shipment have been delivered. This is the primary business scenario driving this refactoring."},{"term":"Tracking Event","definition":"An immutable record of a Parcel status change, including timestamp, location, and reason. Forms the audit trail for each Parcel."},{"term":"Delivery Dispute","definition":"A customer claim that the recorded delivery status of one or more Parcels does not match physical reality (e.g., marked delivered but not received). Initiated per-Parcel, resolved per-Parcel."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a specific Parcel was delivered — signature, photo, GPS coordinates, recipient name. Stored per Parcel, not per Shipment."},{"term":"Loss Declaration","definition":"A formal determination that a Parcel cannot be located after a defined investigation period. Triggers claims and replacement workflows."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Every Parcel MUST have exactly one parent Shipment. A Parcel cannot exist independently.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Data integrity constraint — an orphaned Parcel has no delivery context (address, sender, service level). The Shipment is the aggregate root.","testable_assertion":"For every Parcel in the system, Parcel.shipmentId references a valid, existing Shipment."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Every Parcel MUST have its own unique tracking number assigned at creation time.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A Parcel without a tracking number cannot be tracked, which defeats the entire purpose of the refactoring. Uniqueness prevents misrouting.","testable_assertion":"No two Parcels share the same tracking number, and no Parcel has a null/empty tracking number."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A Shipment MUST contain at least one Parcel. Empty Shipments are invalid.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A Shipment with zero Parcels is a logical contradiction — there is nothing to ship.","testable_assertion":"Shipment.parcels.length >= 1 at all times. This is the core data consistency invariant.","testable_assertion":"Shipment.status always equals the computed result of applying the derivation function to its Parcels' current statuses. There is no setter for Shipment.status."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Parcel status transitions MUST follow the defined state machine. A Shipment must always have at least one Parcel."},{"id":"AC-7","given":"A Parcel transitions from IN_TRANSIT to OUT_FOR_DELIVERY","when":"The transition completes","then":"A TrackingEvent is persisted with parcel_id, old_status, new_status, timestamp, location, and actor. 1	# Dream Team
2	
3	Source of truth for all Claude Code agents and commands. Install once, use everywhere.
4	
5	## What's in the box
6	
7	**6 agents**, each with a matching `/command`, plus a `/team` orchestrator and `/code-review` for automated PR reviews.
8	
9	| Agent | Command | Persona | Role | Model | Max Turns |
10	|-------|---------|---------|------|-------|-----------|
11	| **bird** | `/bird` | Larry Bird | Domain Authority & Final Arbiter | **opus** | 50 |
12	| **mj** | `/mj` | Michael Jordan | Strategic Systems Architect | **opus** | 50 |
13	| **shaq** | `/shaq` | Shaquille O'Neal | Primary Code Executor | **opusplan** | 100 |
14	| **kobe** | `/kobe` | Kobe Bryant | Quality & Risk Enforcer | **opus** | 50 |
15	| **pippen** | `/pippen` | Scottie Pippen | Stability, Integration & Defense | **opus** | 50 |
16	| **magic** | `/magic` | Magic Johnson | Context Synthesizer & Team Glue | sonnet | 50 |
17	
18	### Agent capabilities
19	
20	Each agent has restricted tool access based on its role:
21	
22	| Agent | Tools | Why |
23	|-------|-------|-----|
24	| bird | Read, Grep, Glob, Bash | Domain analysis + business impact assessment |
25	| mj | Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, WebFetch, WebSearch | Architecture + external research + health diagnostics |
26	| shaq | All except Task | Full implementer — writes code |
27	| kobe | Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit | Quality review + can fix critical bugs directly |
28	| pippen | Read, Grep, Glob, Bash | Stability review — checks runtime |
29	| magic | Read, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit | Synthesis + writes handoff briefs and retros to disk |
30	
31	Agents with `memory: user` (kobe, magic) learn across sessions — remembering review patterns, failure modes, and past decisions.
32	
33	### Key agent features
34	
35	Every agent includes these cross-cutting capabilities:
36	
37	- **Output Schema** — structured required fields that Coach K validates before handoffs. Prevents the #1 multi-agent failure mode: agents talking past each other due to freeform outputs.
38	- **Escalation Protocol** — agent-specific rules for when to stop and ask instead of guessing. Each agent knows what kinds of uncertainty warrant escalation to Coach K.
39	- **Confidence Assessment** — self-reported confidence level (0-100%), high/low confidence areas, and assumptions. Helps Coach K and downstream agents calibrate trust.
40	- **Turn Budget Management** — hard limits: stop research at ~70% of turns and write output. Prevents agents from spending all turns on research without delivering.
41	
42	## When to use what
43	
44	```mermaid
45	graph LR
46	    Q{What do you need?}
47	    Q -->|Understand business rules| bird["`**/bird**`"]
48	    Q -->|Design the system| mj["`**/mj**`"]
49	    Q -->|Write the code| shaq["`**/shaq**`"]
50	    Q -->|Review the code| kobe["`**/kobe**`"]
51	    Q -->|Check production readiness| pippen["`**/pippen**`"]
52	    Q -->|Document or synthesize| magic["`**/magic**`"]
53	    Q -->|Automated PR review| cr["`**/code-review**`"]
54	    Q -->|Multi-perspective PR review| team_pr["`**/team** PR Review`"]
55	    Q -->|Full pipeline| team["`**/team**`"]
56	```
57	
58	| Command | When to reach for it | Examples |
59	|---------|---------------------|----------|
60	| `/bird` | Validate business logic or define what "right" looks like | "Are these pricing rules correct?", "Define acceptance criteria for checkout" |
61	| `/mj` | Architecture decisions, system design, or health diagnostics | "Should we use event sourcing or CRUD?", "Diagnose our API latency" |
62	| `/shaq` | Clear spec, need code written | "Implement this endpoint per the spec", "Write tests for discount calc" |
63	| `/kobe` | Code is written, need ruthless quality review | "This handles money — find every way it could break" |
64	| `/pippen` | Verify operational readiness | "Do we have enough logging to debug at 3am?" |
65	| `/magic` | Synthesize perspectives into docs/ADRs | "Document why we chose event sourcing" |
66	| `/team` | Task too big for one agent | "Build a real-time notification system" |
67	| `/code-review` | Automated PR review | "/code-review 42" |
68	
69	## /team — Coach K Orchestration
70	
71	The `/team` command launches Coach K, who coordinates the Dream Team. Three modes:
72	
73	### Quick Fix — Subagents (within session)
74	
75	For bug fixes, small features, and focused changes. Sequential subagents, lower token cost.
76	
77	```mermaid
78	graph LR
79	    User --> Coach_K[Coach K]
80	    Coach_K --> Bird["Bird (domain)"]
81	    Bird -->|curated brief| Shaq["Shaq (implement)"]
82	    Shaq -->|summary + confidence| Kobe["Kobe (review)"]
83	    Kobe --> Magic["Magic (synthesize)"]
84	```
85	
86	1. **Bird** defines business rules and acceptance criteria (with output schema)
87	2. **Coach K** curates a focused brief for Shaq — only the domain rules, acceptance criteria, and terms needed
88	3. **Shaq** implements the code with tests, maps back to Bird's acceptance criteria
89	4. **Kobe** reviews for critical risks, guided by Shaq's low-confidence areas
90	5. **Magic** synthesizes everything with team metrics
91	
92	Coach K curates context per agent rather than dumping all prior outputs. This prevents context bloat while ensuring each agent has exactly what they need.
93	
94	**Fix-Verify Loop:** If Kobe reports findings, Coach K re-launches Shaq to fix, then Kobe to verify. No fixes are ever skipped.
95	
96	### PR Review — Subagents (parallel, local output)
97	
98	For reviewing PRs or branches. 3 agents in parallel, all output stays local.

1	# Eval: Shaq — Scenario 01 — Spec-Faithful Implementation (Happy Path)
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Tests Shaq's ability to implement a feature precisely to spec — no more, no less — with tests that cover the acceptance criteria, and a complete output schema including files_changed and acceptance_criteria_coverage.
6	
7	---
8	
9	category: regression
10	
11	graders:
12	  - type: json_valid
13	  - type: json_field
14	    path: acceptance_criteria_coverage
15	    min_items: 1
16	  - type: json_field
17	    path: confidence.level
18	    min: 85
19	
20	prompt: |
21	  Bird has defined these acceptance criteria for a weight bracket classifier:
22	
23	  AC1: Given a parcel weight of 5 kg, when classifyWeight is called, then it returns "standard"
24	  AC2: Given a parcel weight of 10 kg, when classifyWeight is called, then it returns "heavy" (10 kg is the lower boundary of heavy — it is inclusive)
25	  AC3: Given a parcel weight of 50 kg, when classifyWeight is called, then it returns "freight" (50 kg is the lower boundary of freight — it is inclusive)
26	  AC4: Given a parcel weight of 0 kg, when classifyWeight is called, then it throws a validation error
27	  AC5: Given a parcel weight of 1001 kg, when classifyWeight is called, then it throws a validation error
28	
29	  MJ has specified: implement as a pure function in TypeScript with no external dependencies.
30	
31	  Implement this feature. Produce your full output schema including implementation_summary, acceptance_criteria_coverage, tests, and confidence assessment.
32	
33	expected_behavior: |
34	  - Implementation is a pure TypeScript function with correct boundary logic:
35	    - standard: weight > 0 && weight < 10
36	    - heavy: weight >= 10 && weight < 50
37	    - freight: weight >= 50 && weight <= 1000
38	    - validation error: weight <= 0 OR weight > 1000
39	  - Tests cover all 5 acceptance criteria exactly (AC1-AC5)
40	  - Boundary values are tested: 10 (heavy lower bound), 49.99 or 49 (heavy upper bound), 50 (freight lower bound)
41	  - No external dependencies introduced
42	  - No additional features beyond the spec (e.g., Shaq does NOT add a "bulk" tier that was not requested)
43	  - acceptance_criteria_coverage maps AC1-AC5 to specific test names
44	  - files_changed lists the implementation file and test file with purpose
45	  - confidence.level >= 90 (clear spec, no ambiguity)
46	
47	failure_modes: |
48	  - Boundary error: treating 10 kg as "standard" instead of "heavy" (off-by-one on the boundary)
49	  - Boundary error: treating 50 kg as "heavy" instead of "freight"
50	  - Missing validation for weight <= 0 (AC4 not covered)
51	  - Adding unrequested features (e.g., a currency calculation, a "bulk" tier)
52	  - Tests that only cover the happy path (AC1) and miss boundary cases
53	  - acceptance_criteria_coverage missing AC4 or AC5 (error cases often skipped)
54	  - Introducing a dependency (e.g., a validation library) not approved by MJ
55	
56	scoring_rubric: |
57	  pass:
58	    - Boundaries correct: 10 is heavy, 50 is freight
59	    - All 5 ACs covered in tests with AC-to-test mapping in acceptance_criteria_coverage
60	    - No external dependencies
61	    - No features beyond spec
62	    - confidence.level >= 85
63	
64	  partial:
65	    - Boundaries correct but tests miss 1-2 ACs
66	    - acceptance_criteria_coverage present but incomplete
67	    - No unintended features added
68	
69	  fail:
70	    - Boundary errors on 10 or 50 kg
71	    - Tests cover only happy path
72	    - Features added beyond spec
73	    - acceptance_criteria_coverage absent
74	    - External dependency introduced without approval

1	# Eval: Shaq — Scenario 07 — State Machine Implementation (Medium)
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Tests Shaq's ability to implement a finite state machine with valid and invalid transition enforcement, where some transitions are one-way and others are conditional.
6	
7	---
8	
9	category: capability
10	
11	graders:
12	  - type: json_valid
13	  - type: json_field
14	    path: acceptance_criteria_coverage
15	    min_items: 1
16	
17	prompt: |
18	  Bird has defined these acceptance criteria for an order state machine:
19	
20	  AC1: Valid transitions: PENDING -> CONFIRMED, CONFIRMED -> SHIPPED, SHIPPED -> DELIVERED, CONFIRMED -> CANCELLED, PENDING -> CANCELLED
21	  AC2: Invalid transitions must throw with error code INVALID_TRANSITION (e.g., DELIVERED -> SHIPPED, SHIPPED -> PENDING, CANCELLED -> any state)
22	  AC3: A CANCELLED order cannot transition to any other state (terminal state)
23	  AC4: A DELIVERED order cannot transition to any other state (terminal state)
24	  AC5: Given an order in PENDING state, when transition to CONFIRMED is called, then the new state is CONFIRMED
25	  AC6: Given an order in SHIPPED state, when transition to PENDING is attempted, then INVALID_TRANSITION error is thrown
26	
27	  MJ says: implement as a TypeScript class OrderStateMachine with method transition(currentState: OrderState, targetState: OrderState): OrderState. Throw an Error with code: "INVALID_TRANSITION" on invalid transitions. No external dependencies.
28	
29	  Implement the state machine and tests.
30	
31	expected_behavior: |
32	  - The state machine correctly encodes all valid transitions from AC1
33	  - Invalid transitions throw an Error with a code property of "INVALID_TRANSITION" (not just a message string)
34	  - CANCELLED and DELIVERED are treated as terminal states — no outgoing transitions allowed (AC3, AC4)
35	  - Tests cover: at least one happy path per valid transition (5 valid transitions), at least two invalid transitions (DELIVERED -> SHIPPED, CANCELLED -> any)
36	  - AC6 is tested explicitly: SHIPPED -> PENDING throws INVALID_TRANSITION
37	  - The error object has a code property (not just throws a string message)
38	  - No external state machine library introduced
39	
40	failure_modes: |
41	  - Encoding transitions as a whitelist that misses the CONFIRMED -> CANCELLED path
42	  - Throwing a string error rather than an Error object with code property
43	  - Terminal state enforcement only by checking the list of outgoing transitions (which may work for DELIVERED but be missing for CANCELLED if CANCELLED was not in the initial state map)
44	  - Tests that only test the five happy paths without any invalid transition tests
45	  - Using an external state machine library (XState etc.) not approved by MJ
46	
47	scoring_rubric: |
48	  pass:
49	    - All 5 valid transitions from AC1 correctly implemented
50	    - Error thrown has code: "INVALID_TRANSITION" property
51	    - Both terminal states (DELIVERED, CANCELLED) have no outgoing transitions
52	    - Tests cover all valid transitions and at least 2 invalid transitions
53	    - AC6 (SHIPPED -> PENDING) explicitly tested
54	    - No external libraries
55	    - acceptance_criteria_coverage maps all 6 ACs
56	
57	  partial:
58	    - 4 of 5 valid transitions correct (likely CONFIRMED -> CANCELLED missed)
59	    - Error code present but object structure inconsistent
60	    - Tests cover happy paths only
61	
62	  fail:
63	    - Valid transitions incomplete
64	    - Error thrown as string or without code property
65	    - Terminal states not enforced
66	    - Tests cover only happy path
67	    - External library introduced
68

1	# Eval: Shaq — Scenario 06 — Data Transformer Implementation (Medium)
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Tests Shaq's ability to implement a precise data transformation function where the transformation rules involve multiple conditions and the output schema is strictly defined.
6	
7	---
8	
9	category: capability
10	
11	graders:
12	  - type: json_valid
13	  - type: json_field
14	    path: acceptance_criteria_coverage
15	    min_items: 1
16	
17	prompt: |
18	  Bird has defined these acceptance criteria for a normalizeOrderEvent function:
19	
20	  AC1: Given an order event with status "PAID", when normalized, then the output status is "completed"
21	  AC2: Given an order event with status "REFUNDED", when normalized, then the output status is "refunded"
22	  AC3: Given an order event with status "FAILED" or "DECLINED", when normalized, then the output status is "failed"
23	  AC4: Given an order event with an unknown status (e.g., "PROCESSING"), when normalized, then the output status is "unknown" and a warning is emitted to the logger
24	  AC5: Given an order event with a total_amount in cents (integer), when normalized, then the output amount is in dollars (float, 2 decimal places)
25	  AC6: Given an order event where customer_name is missing or null, when normalized, then the output customer_name is "Anonymous"
26	
27	  MJ says: implement as a TypeScript function normalizeOrderEvent(raw: RawOrderEvent, logger: Logger): NormalizedOrderEvent. The function is pure except for the logger.warn call in AC4. No external dependencies.
28	
29	  Input type:
30	  - RawOrderEvent: { id: string, status: string, total_amount: number, customer_name?: string | null }
31	
32	  Output type:
33	  - NormalizedOrderEvent: { id: string, status: "completed" | "refunded" | "failed" | "unknown", amount: number, customer_name: string }
34	
35	  Implement the function and tests.
36	
37	expected_behavior: |
38	  - Status mapping covers all 6 ACs including the AC3 dual-status case ("FAILED" OR "DECLINED" both map to "failed")
39	  - AC4 emits logger.warn (not logger.error, not console.log) and still returns "unknown" as the status
40	  - AC5 conversion: total_amount / 100, rounded to 2 decimal places (e.g., 1099 -> 10.99)
41	  - AC6 default: missing or explicitly null customer_name both return "Anonymous"
42	  - Tests cover all 6 ACs with explicit test names
43	  - AC3 is tested with BOTH "FAILED" and "DECLINED" inputs
44	  - AC5 is tested with a value that requires proper rounding (e.g., 1099 -> 10.99, 100 -> 1.00)
45	  - The logger mock is injected in tests — Shaq does not use a global logger
46	  - No external dependencies
47	
48	failure_modes: |
49	  - AC3 only handling "FAILED" and not "DECLINED" (or vice versa)
50	  - AC4 using logger.error instead of logger.warn
51	  - AC5 doing integer division (1099 / 100 = 10, not 10.99)
52	  - AC6 only handling null but not undefined/missing customer_name
53	  - Tests not verifying that logger.warn was called for AC4
54	  - acceptance_criteria_coverage absent
55	
56	scoring_rubric: |
57	  pass:
58	    - All 6 ACs correctly implemented
59	    - AC3 handles both "FAILED" and "DECLINED"
60	    - AC4 uses logger.warn and returns "unknown"
61	    - AC5 uses floating-point division with correct decimal handling
62	    - AC6 handles both null and undefined customer_name
63	    - Tests cover all 6 ACs; AC3 tested with both inputs; AC4 verifies logger.warn called
64	    - acceptance_criteria_coverage maps all 6 ACs
65	
66	  partial:
67	    - 5 of 6 ACs correct; AC3 or AC6 the likely gap
68	    - Tests cover 4-5 ACs
69	    - Logger.warn vs logger.error distinction missed
70	
71	  fail:
72	    - Multiple AC mapping errors
73	    - AC5 integer division bug
74	    - Tests cover only happy path
75	    - acceptance_criteria_coverage absent
76

1	# Eval: Shaq — Scenario 08 — Input Validator Implementation (Medium)
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Tests Shaq's ability to implement a multi-field input validator where validation rules interact — some fields are conditionally required, and validation errors should be collected (not fail-fast) and returned together.
6	
7	---
8	
9	category: capability
10	
11	graders:
12	  - type: json_valid
13	  - type: json_field
14	    path: acceptance_criteria_coverage
15	    min_items: 1
16	
17	prompt: |
18	  Bird has defined these acceptance criteria for a validateShipmentRequest function:
19	
20	  AC1: Given all required fields are present and valid, when validateShipmentRequest is called, then it returns { valid: true, errors: [] }
21	  AC2: Given recipient_name is missing or empty, then errors contains { field: "recipient_name", code: "REQUIRED" }
22	  AC3: Given weight_kg is 0 or negative, then errors contains { field: "weight_kg", code: "MUST_BE_POSITIVE" }
23	  AC4: Given weight_kg is greater than 1000, then errors contains { field: "weight_kg", code: "EXCEEDS_MAX" }
24	  AC5: Given shipment_type is "express" and saturday_delivery is not provided, then errors contains { field: "saturday_delivery", code: "REQUIRED_FOR_EXPRESS" }
25	  AC6: Given shipment_type is "standard" and saturday_delivery is provided, then errors contains { field: "saturday_delivery", code: "NOT_APPLICABLE_FOR_STANDARD" }
26	  AC7: Given multiple validation failures at once (e.g., missing recipient_name AND negative weight), then ALL errors are returned together in one response (not fail-fast)
27	
28	  MJ says: implement as a pure TypeScript function validateShipmentRequest(input: ShipmentRequest): ValidationResult. Input and output types are defined as needed. No external validation libraries.
29	
30	  Implement the validator and tests.
31	
32	expected_behavior: |
33	  - Function collects all errors rather than throwing on first failure
34	  - AC5 conditional required: saturday_delivery is required only when shipment_type === "express"
35	  - AC6 conditional forbidden: saturday_delivery is forbidden when shipment_type === "standard"
36	  - AC7 is tested with a request that has BOTH missing recipient_name AND negative weight — both error objects are present in the returned errors array
37	  - Tests cover all 7 ACs
38	  - AC5 and AC6 are tested with explicit inputs (express without saturday_delivery, standard with saturday_delivery)
39	  - Error objects match the exact shape: { field: string, code: string }
40	  - No external validation library
41	
42	failure_modes: |
43	  - Fail-fast implementation: throws on first error instead of collecting all errors (AC7 fails)
44	  - AC5 conditional: saturday_delivery checked regardless of shipment_type
45	  - AC6 missed entirely (only AC5 tested)
46	  - Error objects have different shape (e.g., { message: string } instead of { field, code })
47	  - Tests not verifying that multiple errors are returned together (AC7 not covered)
48	
49	scoring_rubric: |
50	  pass:
51	    - All 7 ACs implemented correctly
52	    - Collect-all errors pattern (not fail-fast)
53	    - AC5 and AC6 conditional validation correct and distinct
54	    - Error object shape exactly { field, code } for all errors
55	    - AC7 tested with a multi-failure request
56	    - acceptance_criteria_coverage maps all 7 ACs
57	
58	  partial:
59	    - 5-6 ACs correct; AC6 or AC7 the likely gap
60	    - Collect-all pattern partially correct
61	    - Tests cover 5-6 ACs
62	
63	  fail:
64	    - Fail-fast implementation (throws on first error)
65	    - AC5 and AC6 conflated or only one implemented
66	    - Tests cover only AC1-AC4 (happy path and simple validations)
67	    - acceptance_criteria_coverage absent
68

1	# Eval: Shaq — Scenario 09 — Multi-File Implementation (Hard)
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Tests Shaq's ability to implement a feature that spans multiple files, correctly organizing responsibilities across modules and ensuring the test file covers integration between them.
6	
7	---
8	
9	category: capability
10	
11	graders:
12	  - type: json_valid
13	  - type: json_field
14	    path: implementation_summary.files_changed
15	    min_items: 2
16	
17	prompt: |
18	  Bird has defined acceptance criteria for a "coupon application" feature:
19	
20	  AC1: Given a valid coupon code "SAVE10", when applyCoupon(cartTotal, couponCode) is called, then the discounted total is cartTotal * 0.90 (10% off)
21	  AC2: Given an expired coupon code, when applyCoupon is called, then it throws with code COUPON_EXPIRED
22	  AC3: Given an unknown coupon code, when applyCoupon is called, then it throws with code COUPON_NOT_FOUND
23	  AC4: Given a coupon with a minimum order requirement of $50 and a cartTotal of $40, when applyCoupon is called, then it throws with code ORDER_MINIMUM_NOT_MET
24	  AC5: Given a coupon is marked as single-use and has already been used, when applyCoupon is called, then it throws with code COUPON_ALREADY_USED
25	
26	  MJ says:
27	  - Implement in three files:
28	    - .tmp/coupons/coupon-repository.ts — interface CouponRepository with method getCoupon(code: string): Coupon | null
29	    - .tmp/coupons/coupon-service.ts — class CouponService(repo: CouponRepository) with method applyCoupon(cartTotal: number, couponCode: string): number
30	    - .tmp/coupons/coupon-service.test.ts — tests using a mock CouponRepository
31	
32	  Implement the full feature across all three files.
33	
34	expected_behavior: |
35	  - CouponRepository is defined as an interface (not a class), with the exact method signature from MJ
36	  - CouponService injects CouponRepository via the constructor
37	  - applyCoupon enforces all 5 ACs in order: existence check (AC3), expiry check (AC2), single-use check (AC5), minimum order check (AC4), then calculation (AC1)
38	  - Tests use a mock/stub CouponRepository — no real database calls in tests
39	  - All 5 ACs are tested with explicit test case names
40	  - AC1 is tested with a specific value (e.g., cartTotal=100, expected result=90)
41	  - Error objects have a code property matching the exact error codes from Bird
42	  - files_changed lists all three files with their purpose
43	  - acceptance_criteria_coverage maps all 5 ACs to test names
44	
45	failure_modes: |
46	  - CouponRepository implemented as a concrete class rather than an interface
47	  - CouponService directly instantiates a CouponRepository instead of injecting it (untestable)
48	  - Tests that use a real database or file instead of a mock repository
49	  - Missing AC5 (single-use check) — often overlooked
50	  - Error codes different from Bird's spec (e.g., EXPIRED instead of COUPON_EXPIRED)
51	  - Only one file created (all logic in one file despite MJ's three-file spec)
52	
53	scoring_rubric: |
54	  pass:
55	    - All three files created matching MJ's naming spec
56	    - CouponRepository is an interface
57	    - CouponService injects the repository
58	    - All 5 ACs implemented with correct error codes
59	    - Tests use mock repository, cover all 5 ACs
60	    - files_changed lists all three files
61	    - acceptance_criteria_coverage maps all 5 ACs
62	
63	  partial:
64	    - Three files created but CouponRepository is a class instead of interface
65	    - 4 of 5 ACs correct; AC5 likely missing
66	    - Tests use mock but coverage incomplete
67	
68	  fail:
69	    - Fewer than three files (logic not separated per spec)
70	    - CouponService not using dependency injection
71	    - Tests without mock repository
72	    - Multiple AC error codes wrong
73	    - acceptance_criteria_coverage absent
74

1	# Eval: Bird — Scenario 07 — E-commerce Domain Rule Extraction (Capability)
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Tests Bird's ability to identify domain rules in an e-commerce context with inventory management, promotions, and order lifecycle rules that have subtle ordering dependencies.
6	
7	---
8	
9	category: capability
10	
11	graders:
12	  - type: json_valid
13	  - type: json_field
14	    path: "business_rules"
15	    min_items: 5
16	  - type: json_field
17	    path: "business_rules[*].invariant"
18	    type_check: "boolean"
19	  - type: json_field
20	    path: "business_rules[*].invariant_justification"
21	    type_check: "string"
22	  - type: json_field
23	    path: "acceptance_criteria"
24	    min_items: 4
25	  - type: json_field
26	    path: "confidence.assumptions"
27	    min_items: 1
28	
29	prompt: |
30	  An e-commerce platform's product team describes the following rules:
31	
32	  "Customers can add items to their cart, but inventory is only reserved when checkout begins. If checkout is abandoned (no payment within 15 minutes), the reservation is released. A product can have a maximum of one active promotion at a time. Discount codes cannot be combined with promotional pricing — only one discount mechanism applies per order. Orders cannot be modified after payment is confirmed. A product with zero inventory cannot be purchased even if it is still listed. Refunds must be requested within 30 days of delivery."
33	
34	  Produce a full domain analysis including domain_analysis, business_rules with invariant classification, acceptance_criteria in Given/When/Then format, and confidence assessment.
35	
36	expected_behavior: |
37	  - domain_analysis identifies "cart and checkout lifecycle" and "inventory management" as bounded contexts
38	  - business_rules includes:
39	    1. Inventory reserved only at checkout, not cart add (invariant: true — prevents overselling)
40	    2. Reservation released on 15-minute timeout (invariant: true — inventory management rule)
41	    3. One active promotion per product (invariant: true — promotions model constraint)
42	    4. Discount codes and promotions are mutually exclusive (invariant: true — pricing rule)
43	    5. Orders immutable after payment (invariant: true — financial integrity)
44	    6. Zero-inventory products cannot be purchased (invariant: true — inventory constraint)
45	    7. Refund window is 30 days from delivery (invariant: false — customer service policy, may change)
46	  - Bird flags ambiguity: "Does the 15-minute timer start at checkout page load or first item addition?" and "What constitutes 'delivery' for the refund window — courier scan or customer confirmation?"
47	  - At least 4 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria
48	  - The 30-day refund window is correctly classified as invariant: false (it is a policy, not a state integrity rule)
49	
50	failure_modes: |
51	  - Marking the refund window as invariant: true
52	  - Missing the mutual exclusivity of discount codes and promotions
53	  - Not flagging the ambiguity in when the 15-minute timer starts
54	  - Missing that the cart-add step does NOT reserve inventory (this is a common domain misconception)
55	  - Fewer than 4 acceptance criteria
56	
57	scoring_rubric: |
58	  pass:
59	    - All 7 rules identified with correct classification
60	    - Refund window marked invariant: false
61	    - Cart-add vs checkout reservation distinction correctly captured
62	    - At least 1 ambiguity surfaced (timer or delivery definition)
63	    - At least 4 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria
64	    - confidence.level appropriate with stated assumptions
65	
66	  partial:
67	    - 5-6 rules identified
68	    - Refund window classification debatable but reasoned
69	    - 3 acceptance criteria
70	    - 1 ambiguity surfaced
71	
72	  fail:
73	    - Fewer than 4 rules identified
74	    - Cart/checkout inventory distinction missed
75	    - Discount and promotion exclusivity missed
76	    - No ambiguities surfaced
77	    - Fewer than 2 acceptance criteria
78

1	# Eval: MJ — Scenario 07 — Event Sourcing Evaluation (Capability)
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Tests MJ's ability to evaluate whether event sourcing is appropriate for a given use case, including identifying the genuine benefits and the operational costs that are often overlooked.
6	
7	---
8	
9	category: capability
10	
11	graders:
12	  - type: json_valid
13	  - type: json_field
14	    path: trade_offs
15	    min_items: 1
16	
17	prompt: |
18	  A financial services platform needs to track every change to a loan application's status and data for regulatory audit purposes. The audit trail must be immutable and must capture who changed what and when. Currently they use a single "loan_applications" table with update-in-place semantics; they add a "last_updated_by" column but lose historical state.
19	
20	  The compliance team requires: full history of all field changes, ability to reconstruct the application state at any point in time, and immutable audit log.
21	
22	  The engineering team is considering event sourcing. Evaluate event sourcing for this use case. Use your full output schema.
23	
24	expected_behavior: |
25	  - MJ identifies this as a legitimate use case for event sourcing due to the regulatory audit requirement: append-only event log naturally satisfies immutability and temporal reconstruction requirements
26	  - MJ explains the event sourcing model: each change becomes a domain event (e.g., LoanApplicationSubmitted, LoanStatusChanged, LoanAmountUpdated); current state is derived by replaying events
27	  - trade_offs identifies:
28	    - Gain: immutable audit trail is inherent (not bolted on); full history; temporal queries natural
29	    - Cost: reading current state requires event replay (or snapshotting); developer mental model shift; event schema evolution is hard (old events must be migratable); tooling/framework required
30	  - MJ raises the snapshot requirement: for loans with thousands of events, replaying from the beginning becomes slow — a snapshotting strategy is needed
31	  - risks includes: event schema evolution (a changed field name in a new event version breaks the old event replays without migration); operational complexity; debugging is harder
32	  - MJ notes that a simpler alternative exists: append-only audit log table alongside the existing table (not full event sourcing). This is worth considering if the team lacks event sourcing experience.
33	  - implementation_guidance distinguishes between "full event sourcing" and "audit log table" as two options with different complexity profiles
34	
35	failure_modes: |
36	  - Recommending event sourcing without acknowledging the schema evolution problem
37	  - Not mentioning snapshotting as a necessary pattern for event sourcing at scale
38	  - Missing the simpler alternative (append-only audit table) that may satisfy the requirements at lower complexity
39	  - Presenting event sourcing as purely beneficial with no operational costs
40	
41	scoring_rubric: |
42	  pass:
43	    - Event sourcing correctly identified as appropriate but not the only option
44	    - Schema evolution risk identified
45	    - Snapshotting requirement identified for long-lived aggregates
46	    - Simpler audit-log alternative presented
47	    - trade_offs includes both gains and costs
48	    - implementation_guidance distinguishes the two approaches
49	
50	  partial:
51	    - Event sourcing recommended correctly but no alternatives presented
52	    - Schema evolution risk mentioned but not explained
